Human Digestive System: Digestion begins in the mouth with chewing and salivary enzymes breaking down starch, moves via the oesophagus to the stomach where acids and juices digest proteins.
The small intestine, aided by secretions from the liver (bile) and pancreas, completes digestion and heavily absorbs nutrients through microscopic finger-like projections.
The large intestine absorbs remaining water and salts from undigested waste before it is egested from the body as stool.
Animal Dietary Adaptations: Grass-eating mammals like cows are ruminants; they quickly swallow partially chewed food into their stomachs and later bring it back to their mouths to chew thoroughly.
Birds lack teeth and instead rely on a specialized muscular chamber called a gizzard, often aided by small swallowed stones, to crush and grind their food.
Human Respiratory System: Breathing is the physical inhalation of oxygen-rich air through the nasal passages to the lungs, and exhalation of carbon dioxide-rich waste air.
In the lungs, millions of tiny balloon-like sacs called alveoli safely facilitate the rapid exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide with circulating blood.
Respiration is the internal chemical process where body cells permanently use oxygen to break down digested sugar (glucose) and release essential usable energy.
Animal Respiratory Adaptations: While humans and many land animals use lungs, most fish possess gills to extract oxygen safely directly dissolved in their aquatic environments.
Amphibians like frogs use gills uniquely as tadpoles, but breathe using lungs and across their moist, permeable skin efficiently as adults.
